images.caption: Figure 1:Motivation ofPerceptionRubrics.Top:An existing benchmark favors GPT-4o despite key omissions, while humans prefer responses that capture more perceptually important details.Bottom:Compared with DetailCaps and DOCCI,PerceptionRubricsmore clearly distinguishes model capabilities.

Figure 2:Rubric Demonstration ofPerceptionRubrics.Representative examples are selected for each task, highlighting “Must Right” (; essential features) and “Easy Wrong” pitfalls (; error-prone fine-grained details).
Figure 3:Benchmark Statistics ofPerceptionRubrics:The distribution of tasks across 7 main categories.

Figure 4:ThePerceptionRubricsConstruction Pipeline.Adopting a caption-centric approach, we first synthesize golden captions via circular peer-review (Top). These captions then serve as anchors to generate Must-Right and Easy-Wrong rubrics through domain-specific prompting (Bottom).
Figure 5:Distribution of golden caption lengths in our benchmark.The histogram shows the word count frequency across the dataset.

Figure 6:Comprehensive Failure Analysis.(Left)Distribution of error sources across different models.(Right)Reliability Gap Analysis comparing Atomic Accuracy (the average pass rate over individual rubrics) with the stricter Must-Right-All-Pass Rate, highlighting the difficulty of maintaining consistency across all constraints.
Figure 7:Correlation Analysisbetween basic perceptual reliability (Must-Right) and fine-grained understanding (Easy-Wrong) across six representative models.
Figure 8:Rubric Coverage vs. Evaluation Stability.As the sampled rubric ratio increases from20%20\%to80%80\%, the standard deviation of model scores decreases.

Figure 9:Alignment with Human Preference.We compare benchmark scores from DOCCI(Onoeet al.,2024), DetailCaps(Donget al.,2024), andPerceptionRubricsagainst human preference scores from Vision Arena for the five overlapping models.
Each point denotes one model.PerceptionRubricsshows the strongest correlation with Vision Arena, achieving Pearson0.9160.916and Spearman1.0001.000.

Figure 11:Distribution analysis of rubrics. (a) Frequency distribution of the total rubrics count across the dataset. (b) Probability density comparison of rubrics count betweenMust-RightandEasy-Wrongcategories.

header: Appendix BModel Roles and Pipeline Details

images.caption: Figure 12:Qualitative examples of the fine-grained rubrics across four categories: Natural Scene, Document & OCR, Digital UI & UX, and Structured Data. Each example consists of an image and two tiers of rubrics:Must-Right(top group) focusing on core facts, andEasy-Wrong(bottom group) focusing on challenging details, negative constraints, and logical reasoning.

Figure 13:Qualitative examples of the fine-grained rubrics across three additional categories: Logic & Puzzle, STEM & Expert, and Creative & Cultural. Each example consists of an image and two tiers of rubrics:Must-Right(top group) focusing on core facts, andEasy-Wrong(bottom group) focusing on challenging details, negative constraints, and logical reasoning.
